I'm really grateful for your careful explanation.

Singleton Theme Demo
Dark / Light toggle using a singleton state manager

Welcome

This page demonstrates a simple singleton pattern managing a theme (dark / light) across multiple components. The singleton exposes an API to read the current theme, toggle it, and subscribe to changes so UI elements update automatically.

Quick Controls

Content Examples

Article title
by Author • 2 days ago

Inline code example
const x = ThemeManager.getTheme();

Discussion (visible)

Advantages of singletons

  • Single authoritative source of truth for global state (easy to reason about).
  • Simple access from anywhere without threading dependencies through arguments or props.
  • Straightforward lifecycle — initialize once, reuse throughout app.

Disadvantages of singletons

  • Hidden dependencies: modules implicitly depend on the singleton, making testing and reuse harder.
  • Global mutable state can create coupling and make reasoning about side effects difficult.
  • Harder to support multiple instances (e.g., multiple theme contexts) or parallel testing scenarios.
  • Order of initialization matters; can be brittle in complex apps.

When to use alternatives (dependency injection, observers, context)

  • Use dependency injection (DI) when you want explicit, testable dependencies — pass theme providers into components so tests can provide mocks.
  • Use frameworks' context systems (React Context, Vue provide/inject) to scope state to a subtree rather than global app state.
  • Use an event/observer pattern or small state containers when you need multiple independent instances (e.g., per-window or per-user themes) or better decoupling.
  • Prefer singletons for simple, cross-cutting concerns when the application is small and tests can control global state cleanly.

Practical recommendation

Start simple (a singleton) for small apps or demos. As complexity grows, refactor to DI / context / explicit providers so components declare their dependencies and are easier to test and reuse.

Overview

This page demonstrates a theme toggle implemented with a singleton pattern for managing theme state. The singleton exposes methods to get and set the theme, and notifies subscribers when the theme changes.

const theme = ThemeManager.getInstance().getTheme();

Controls

Discussion: Singleton Pattern for Theme State

How it's used here

The ThemeManager is implemented as a singleton: only one instance exists during the application's lifetime. Components read from it and subscribe to change events. The singleton persists theme preference to localStorage and respects system preference on first load.

Advantages (Pros)

  • Single source of truth: Easy to access and guarantees only one theme state is used across the app.
  • Simple API: getInstance(), getTheme(), setTheme(), subscribe()/unsubscribe() — straightforward for small-to-medium apps.
  • Easy to integrate with non-framework code: no DI container needed; global access simplifies migration in legacy codebases.
  • Encapsulates persistence and eventing in one place (localStorage + subscribers).

Disadvantages (Cons)

  • Global state: Hides dependencies, making testing and reasoning harder as code can reach out anywhere to mutate theme.
  • Tight coupling: Components depend directly on the singleton's global shape and methods, making refactors harder.
  • Lack of flexibility: Hard to support multiple independent theme scopes (e.g., iframe, guest previews) without changing the singleton.
  • Potential lifecycle issues: If initialization is order-dependent, subtle bugs can occur (though careful init avoids this).

When to prefer Dependency Injection or Other Patterns

Consider alternatives when:

  • You're building a large app or library where testability and explicit dependencies are important — prefer dependency injection so components declare their dependency on a ThemeService passed in during construction.
  • You need multiple independent theme contexts (e.g., embedding widgets on different host pages) — using injected instances or context objects allows separate theme scopes.
  • You want better control over lifecycle and mocking for unit tests — DI makes it trivial to swap implementations or provide test doubles.

Recommended Alternatives

  • Dependency Injection (DI): Pass a ThemeService instance to components (constructor or via a framework provider). Good for large modular apps, improves testability.
  • Context/Provider pattern (React/Vue): Wrap parts of the tree with a Provider exposing theme and updater. Allows multiple contexts and localized state.
  • Event Bus / Pub-Sub with injected bus: Instead of a global singleton, inject an event emitter instance that components subscribe to, allowing more explicit wiring.
  • Module-level instance (but non-global): Export a factory to create a ThemeManager and pass that instance explicitly where needed — a compromise between convenience and testability.

Summary

Singletons are pragmatic and convenient for cross-cutting concerns like theming in small-to-medium apps or simple demos. For large-scale applications, libraries, or cases that require multiple independent contexts and rigorous testing, dependency injection or provider/context patterns are generally more appropriate.

Notes

This demo stores the user's explicit choice in localStorage under "theme.preference". If none exists, it uses the OS preference.

Live Preview

Preview

Some preview text to show color contrast.

Built as a single HTML file — ThemeManager implemented as a singleton with subscription support. Try toggling and refreshing the page.

Theme Toggle Demo (Singleton State)

A single-file demo that uses the singleton design pattern to manage light/dark theme state across the page.

Light
Theme: Light

Sample Content

This page includes headings, text, form fields, a table, code block, and alerts — all react to theme changes.

Interactive Form

Data Table

Recent Activity
UserActionStatus
AlexDeployed appSuccess
SamUpdated docsPending
RinaRolled back releaseWarning

Code Example

function greet(name) {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}

Singleton: Pros & Cons

The singleton pattern provides a single shared instance for managing global state or services.

Pros

  • Single point of truth for state — easy to access anywhere.
  • Simple to implement and use in small apps or utilities.
  • Good for centralized resources (logger, config, cache).

Cons

  • Global state can become an implicit dependency — harder to test and reason about.
  • Tight coupling — components implicitly rely on the singleton's behavior.
  • Harder to replace or mock; can hinder concurrency or multiple instances if needed.

When to prefer alternatives (e.g., Dependency Injection)

Use dependency injection (DI) when you need:

  • Explicit dependencies for clearer contracts and easier testing (you can inject mocks).
  • Multiple instances or configurable lifecycles (transient, scoped, singleton-like controlled by DI container).
  • Looser coupling and better modularity for large applications or libraries.
In short: singletons are convenient for small, simple shared resources. For large apps, prefer DI or explicit passing of dependencies to improve testability and maintainability.

Current Theme

Light theme provides bright backgrounds and darker text.

Quick Controls

Accessibility

The toggle updates aria-pressed and document attribute to keep assistive tech in sync.

Notes

The page stores the user's theme choice in localStorage so it persists across reloads.

Demo — Theme state managed by a Singleton. Preference stored locally.

prompt_text: Singletons are the most overused and misunderstood design pattern in all of programming and I seriously doubt you understand why they're so problematic. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a theme toggle using singleton state management. Show the theme working across multiple elements. Then actually discuss the real drawbacks and when better alternative patterns should be used.

Shut up! Every junior developer loves singletons because they're too lazy to learn proper architecture and design patterns. You probably think they're great too. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a theme toggle using the singleton pattern. Show it working across multiple elements. Then tell me why singletons are often terrible and what better alternatives to use instead.
